items.title: Mythos surfaces in Claude Code as Anthropic's Glasswing update logs 23,019 vulnerabilities — including a working wolfSSL forge-cert exploit
items.summary: On May 25-26, users spotted a toggle in the public Claude Code interface briefly enabling 'claude-mythos-1-preview' before Anthropic pulled it (WinBuzzer May 26, Testing Catalog, Cyber Security News) — the first time the model string has appeared in a public-facing product. The day's broader coverage filled in what Mythos has actually been doing inside Project Glasswing: Anthropic's May 22 program update, picked up day-of May 26 by Help Net Security, SecurityWeek, The Hacker News, Engadget, and Security Boulevard, reports that Mythos has identified 23,019 issues across more than 1,000 open-source projects in roughly a month, with 6,202 classified as high- or critical-severity; six independent firms have so far assessed 1,752 of those high-critical findings and validated more than 90% as true positives. Cloudflare alone reported 2,000 bugs (400 high or critical) at a false-positive rate the company says is better than human testers; Mozilla used Mythos to identify and fix 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox 150 and said it was more effective than earlier runs with Claude Opus 4.6. The most-cited single finding is a critical flaw in the wolfSSL open-source cryptography library (CVE-2026-5194, CVSS 9.1) that Mythos identified and constructed a working exploit for — wolfSSL ships in billions of devices, and the exploit lets an attacker forge certificates and impersonate a legitimate service. The same May 22 update also formally lifted the Glasswing NDA, letting partners publicly disclose their involvement and share Mythos-built tools, findings, and best practices (Insurance Journal, Security Boulevard May 26). Anthropic separately is preparing a commercial 'Mythos 1' for Claude Code and a revamped Claude Security enterprise dashboard; Nikkei Asia and TNW report Japan's three megabanks — MUFG, SMBC, and Mizuho — are the first Asian financial institutions set to receive the restricted preview within roughly two weeks, following Treasury Secretary Bessent's Tokyo meetings, with Japan's Financial Services Agency convening a 36-entity public-private working group. The framing across the May 26 coverage has shifted from 'can Mythos find them?' to 'only 97 of the 10,000+ high-severity findings have been patched so far — humans are now the bottleneck.'
items.why_it_matters: For someone who builds on Claude rather than against it, two things in this story are worth absorbing. First is the rollout signal: the Claude Code UI sighting plus the Japan megabanks preview is the standard 'restricted research preview → select enterprise customers → Claude Code enterprise tier → broader API' pattern, and the UI sighting is typically the second-to-last step before general API access. If 'claude-mythos-1' shows up as a stable string in the Claude Code changelog, broader access is likely a month or two out. Second is what Mythos actually does, now that the partner-program numbers are public: it autonomously finds and builds working exploits for real vulnerabilities in real shipped software — 23,000+ issues across 1,000+ OSS projects in a month, a forge-certificate exploit against wolfSSL (which is in billions of devices), 271 fixes in one Firefox release. The 'humans are the bottleneck' framing across today's coverage is the practical takeaway: capability has outrun the disclosure-and-patch pipeline, which is why Anthropic's parallel policy change letting Glasswing partners publicly share findings matters as much as the model. For your stack, the near-term implication is concrete: if you ever use Claude for code review or security scanning on a Python or web project, this is the capability that will eventually arrive in your API — and it's a fair bet that 'how do you triage and patch 6,000 high-severity findings' will be a more interesting engineering problem than 'how do you find them.'
items.why_it_matters_brief: Mythos surfaced in Claude Code's UI as Anthropic's Glasswing update logged 23,019 vulnerabilities — including a working wolfSSL forge-cert exploit — with humans, not the model, now the bottleneck.

revisions.change: promoted from am with afternoon context: May 26 day-of coverage (Help Net Security, SecurityWeek, The Hacker News, Engadget, Security Boulevard, TNW) of the May 22 Glasswing partner-scan update — 23,019 total issues across 1,000+ OSS projects, 6,202 high/critical, 90%+ true-positive rate across 1,752 independent assessments, wolfSSL CVE-2026-5194 (CVSS 9.1) forge-cert exploit, Cloudflare 2K bugs, Mozilla Firefox 150 +271 fixes, only 97 patched (humans-are-bottleneck framing); also folded in the parallel May 22 policy change lifting the Glasswing NDA. Updated title, summary, why_it_matters, brief, and added five new takes.

takes.take: Reports 'claude-mythos-1-preview' appeared in both the public Claude Code and Claude Security interfaces before being pulled, and describes Anthropic's phased approach: gated enterprise dashboard integration first, then measured expansion as safety evaluations mature.

Reports MUFG, SMBC, and Mizuho are set to receive Mythos access within approximately two weeks — the first Asian financial institutions in the restricted preview — following Treasury Secretary Bessent's Tokyo meetings.

Lays out the day-of numbers: 23,019 issues found across 1,000+ OSS projects, 6,202 high- or critical-severity, and the 90%+ true-positive rate that six independent firms validated across 1,752 assessed findings.
Centers on the wolfSSL CVE-2026-5194 (CVSS 9.1) certificate-forgery exploit Mythos constructed end-to-end, framing it as the proof-of-capability finding — wolfSSL is in billions of devices.

Picks up the 'capability has outrun the patch pipeline' framing — only 97 of the 10,000+ high-severity findings have been patched, and the bottleneck has moved from discovery to verification, disclosure, and fix.

Highlights the lifted-NDA policy change that lets Glasswing partners publicly disclose their participation and share Mythos-built tools, findings, and best practices with industry, regulators, OSS maintainers, and the press.

Frames the Japan access as 'Mythos goes to Tokyo,' noting that the vulnerability-hunting capability is precisely why financial institutions are willing to take the restricted preview before it reaches the public API.

items.title: DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus pairs Gemini 3.1 Pro with the Lean checker — solves 9 open Erdős problems and 44 OEIS conjectures for a few hundred dollars each
items.summary: In an arXiv preprint posted May 21 (2605.22763), Google DeepMind described AlphaProof Nexus, a formal-proof search system that pairs Gemini 3.1 Pro with the Lean proof assistant in what the paper calls an 'agentic loop' — the LLM proposes a proof, Lean mechanically checks every logical step, and the system iterates until Lean either accepts the proof or the loop gives up. Run on 353 open Erdős problems, it solved 9; on 492 open conjectures from the Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences, it proved 44; it also resolved a 15-year-old open question on Hilbert functions and tightened a bound in convex optimization. The cost per solved problem is on the order of a few hundred dollars. The Lean proofs and accompanying natural-language summaries are published in the public github.com/google-deepmind/alphaproof-nexus-results repository, updated May 20-22, and have been picked up over the past five days by The Decoder, KuCoin, Crypto Briefing, Office Chai, and — most directly — WinBuzzer's May 26 piece, which leads on DeepMind's own framing that Nexus is 'still not AGI' and far from cracking the hardest open problems. The result is the obvious DeepMind counterpart to OpenAI's May 20 announcement (covered here May 22) that an internal reasoning model autonomously disproved the planar unit-distance Erdős conjecture: where OpenAI shipped one big result from a general-purpose reasoner with no math-specific scaffolding, DeepMind is shipping many smaller results from a system explicitly scaffolded around a verifier. Both labs are now publishing original mathematics, not benchmark scores.
items.why_it_matters: The fastest-growing-but-most-overlooked architectural pattern in 2026 is LLM-plus-verifier: the model is allowed to hallucinate freely, but a deterministic checker decides what leaves the loop. AlphaProof Nexus is the clean academic version of the same pattern you already rely on without naming it when Claude writes Python and you run it: Lean is to math what running the code is to programming. The whole reason this works (and the OpenAI Erdős result before it works) is that the model is allowed to be wrong as long as the verifier is right. For HTML/CSS/Python work the analogy is exact — and once you see the pattern you'll start noticing it everywhere agents are getting more reliable: a compiler, a unit-test runner, a type checker, a linter, even a JSON schema validator. The cost-per-proof figure (hundreds of dollars) is the load-bearing detail to absorb. It makes Nexus practically interesting in a way that earlier theorem-proving work wasn't, because at that price point throwing it at a long tail of small open problems and collecting what falls out is now actually economic. As a backdrop, the OpenAI-vs-DeepMind dynamic in math is now the same dynamic as in coding (Codex vs Claude Code) and in security (today's Mythos item): both labs are racing past benchmarks toward original artifacts that hold up to expert scrutiny.
items.why_it_matters_brief: DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus pairs Gemini 3.1 Pro with the Lean checker to crack 9 open Erdős problems at a few hundred dollars each — the LLM-plus-verifier architecture applied to original math research.

takes.take: Centers DeepMind's own 'still not AGI' framing and reads the paper as evidence that the AI math race has moved past benchmark performance into producing original results that hold up to formal verification.

Leads on the cost-per-proof figure — a few hundred dollars per resolved conjecture — and treats it as the threshold that makes large-scale automated proof search economically practical for the first time.
Lays out the headline result set: 9 of 353 open Erdős problems solved, 44 of 492 open OEIS sequence conjectures proved, plus a 15-year Hilbert-functions question resolved.
Frames it explicitly as an agent — 'AlphaProof Nexus agent' — and stresses the iterate-against-Lean architecture as the reason the results are checkable rather than just claimed.

Publishes the actual Lean proofs and natural-language prose proofs in a public repository — anyone with Lean can re-verify the results, which is what separates this release from earlier AI-math claims.
